items.title: Ornith 1.0 — open-weight coding models that learn their own RL scaffold
items.summary: Ornith 1.0 is an MIT-licensed family of agentic coding LLMs (9B, 31B, 35B MoE, 397B MoE) whose RL loop writes its own task-specific scaffold instead of using a fixed human-designed harness.

quickFacts.label: Availability
quickFacts.value: Open weights on Hugging Face (including FP8 quantizations)

quickFacts.label: What's new
quickFacts.value: Model and RL scaffold co-evolve during training

faq.q: What does "self-scaffolding" mean in Ornith 1.0?
faq.a: In Ornith 1.0, each reinforcement learning step runs in two stages: the model first reads a coding task and proposes a refined scaffold, then uses that scaffold to generate a solution rollout. The scaffold co-evolves with the policy, so the model effectively writes the harness that guides its own search instead of relying on a hand-built one.

faq.q: How does Ornith 1.0 score on SWE-Bench compared to SWE-Bench Pro?
faq.a: Ornith-1.0-397B reports 82.4% on SWE-Bench Verified and 62.2% on the harder SWE-Bench Pro, per the DeepReinforce announcement. It also reports 77.5% on Terminal-Bench 2.1 (Terminus-2), positioning the flagship as a strong open-source result for agentic coding evaluations.

faq.q: Which Ornith 1.0 size should I run locally?
faq.a: DeepReinforce ships Ornith 1.0 in 9B Dense, 31B Dense, 35B MoE, and 397B MoE variants, with FP8 quantizations for the larger ones. The 9B is aimed at edge or single-GPU use; the 35B MoE keeps active parameters small for cheap inference, and the 397B flagship is the top-scoring open variant for serious agent workloads.

faq.q: Can I use Ornith 1.0 commercially?
faq.a: Yes — Ornith 1.0 weights are released under the MIT license on Hugging Face, which allows commercial use, modification, and redistribution. DeepReinforce has not yet posted pricing for a hosted API, so for now the only deployment path is self-hosting the open weights through vLLM, SGLang, or a similar serving stack.

explainer.tagline: Open-weight agentic coding models that learn to write their own RL scaffold instead of relying on a fixed harness.
explainer.whatIsIt: Ornith 1.0 introduces a self-scaffolding training loop for agentic coding LLMs. DeepReinforce shipped four MIT-licensed variants on Hugging Face — a 9B dense, a 31B dense, a 35B mixture-of-experts, and a 397B MoE flagship — built on Gemma 4 and Qwen 3.5 pretrained checkpoints.
explainer.howItWorks: The training loop runs each reinforcement learning step in two stages. First, the model reads a coding task and proposes a refined scaffold for solving it; second, it uses that scaffold to generate a solution rollout. Across steps, the scaffold co-evolves with the model's policy, so Ornith 1.0 effectively learns the harness instead of inheriting a hand-built one.
explainer.whyItMatters: Open coding models usually inherit a fixed human harness, which caps how far they can self-improve on new tasks. Ornith 1.0 ships an open recipe where the scaffold is itself a training target — and the 397B flagship hits 82.4% on SWE-Bench Verified and 62.2% on the harder SWE-Bench Pro, giving open-source teams a strong agentic coding baseline under MIT.
explainer.forWho: Open-source agent builders and RL researchers

image.alt: Ornith 1.0 announcement banner from DeepReinforce

items.summary: Sam Witteveen walks through Ornith 1.0, DeepReinforce's MIT-licensed coding model family whose RL loop learns its own scaffold — uploaded hours after the 9B–397B weights landed on Hugging Face.

image.alt: Sam Witteveen YouTube thumbnail for the Ornith 1.0 walkthrough

explainer.tagline: A first hands-on look at Ornith 1.0 — DeepReinforce's open-weight coding LLM family that trains its own RL scaffold.
explainer.whatIsIt: Sam Witteveen breaks down Ornith 1.0, the MIT-licensed agentic coding model family DeepReinforce released on June 25, 2026. The lineup spans a 9B dense, a 31B dense, a 35B mixture-of-experts, and a 397B MoE flagship, all built on Gemma 4 and Qwen 3.5 pretrained checkpoints.
explainer.howItWorks: The walkthrough explains the self-scaffolding RL loop at the core of Ornith 1.0: the model first proposes a task-specific scaffold, then uses that scaffold to generate a solution rollout, with both targets co-evolving during training. Sam Witteveen is one of the eight AI YouTubers AI/TLDR tracks for fast, hands-on coverage of open frontier releases.
explainer.whyItMatters: Coding-model news tends to land as a paper plus weights and little else. Ornith 1.0 is open-source and hits 82.4% on SWE-Bench Verified, but most engineers will first meet it through Sam Witteveen's video — a same-day demo of the variants, the RL recipe, and what the scaffold-co-evolution buys over a fixed harness.

items.title: Un-0 — image generator built from coupled oscillators, not a neural net
items.summary: Un-0 generates images by simulating a population of Kuramoto oscillators whose pairwise coupling strengths are the learned parameters. It hits FID 6.74 on ImageNet 64x64 with MIT weights and training code released.

explainer.tagline: Un-0 generates images from a network of coupled oscillators instead of a diffusion or GAN backbone — and the weights are public.
explainer.whatIsIt: Un-0 is a research image generator from Unconventional AI that replaces the usual deep neural net with a population of Kuramoto oscillators — simple math objects from physics that sync up over time. The coupling strengths between every pair of oscillators are the trainable weights. MIT-licensed weights, training scripts, and ablations all ship together on GitHub.
explainer.howItWorks: Each pixel-region maps to an oscillator. During inference, the oscillators are integrated forward in time and their phases get decoded back into image pixels. Training adjusts the pairwise coupling matrix so the steady-state phases reproduce target images. The codebase ships six checkpoints across CIFAR-10 and ImageNet 64x64, runnable on A100, H200, and B200 GPUs.
explainer.whyItMatters: FID 6.74 on ImageNet 64x64 puts Un-0 in the same range as early diffusion models like NCSN and iDDPM — not state of the art, but proof that a non-neural dynamical system can learn to generate at the same quality bar. The maker pitches the approach as a step toward running AI on roughly 1,000x less energy, since coupled-oscillator hardware exists in optics and analog circuits.
explainer.forWho: ML researchers and hardware-software co-design teams looking past pure deep-learning substrates.

image.alt: Mosaic of sample images generated by Un-0 across CIFAR-10 and ImageNet 64x64.

items.title: Lilian Weng: 'Scaling Laws, Carefully' — first new Lil'Log post in 13 months
items.summary: Lilian Weng walks through scaling laws end-to-end: why Kaplan and Chinchilla reached opposite conclusions, how parameter counting and fit region change the answer, and what data scarcity means for the curves now.

explainer.tagline: Lilian Weng returns to Lil'Log after 13 months with a 25-minute walkthrough of scaling laws, Kaplan vs. Chinchilla, and how easily the curves mislead.
explainer.whatIsIt: Lilian Weng's first new Lil'Log post since May 2025 is a long-form survey of neural scaling laws — the empirical curves that predict how training loss falls as model size, dataset size, and compute go up. It covers what the laws actually predict, where they disagree, and what to be careful about before extrapolating.
explainer.howItWorks: The post traces the lineage from Hestness and Rosenfeld through Kaplan et al. 2020 (which said model size should grow far faster than data, N_opt proportional to C^0.73) to Chinchilla 2022 (which said model and data should scale together, N_opt proportional to C^0.5). Lilian Weng shows the gap mostly comes from how embedding parameters are counted and which loss region is fit — small log-log changes blow up at trillion-token scale.
explainer.whyItMatters: Compute-optimal allocation decides whether to spend the next training run on a bigger model or more tokens, and that single call swings cost by orders of magnitude. The post also covers data-limited regimes with repeated tokens, where naive scaling laws break down — directly relevant now that frontier labs are running out of fresh web text.
explainer.forWho: ML researchers, pretraining engineers, and anyone reasoning about the next compute budget.

image.alt: Loss vs compute power-law chart from Kaplan et al., reproduced in Lilian Weng's scaling-laws post.

items.title: GitHub Copilot for Jira — GA brings agent streaming inside Atlassian
items.summary: GitHub Copilot for Jira is generally available, letting teams watch the coding agent run on a Jira ticket and steer it in Jira chat without switching to GitHub.

explainer.tagline: Run the GitHub coding agent on a Jira ticket, watch it stream, and steer it from Jira chat.
explainer.whatIsIt: GitHub Copilot for Jira is now generally available. The Atlassian Marketplace app links a Jira ticket to a GitHub coding agent session, so progress streams into Jira and follow-up instructions in the Jira chat keep the agent working on the same pull request.
explainer.howItWorks: Inside a Jira issue, the integration shows the agent's live steps as it runs and posts the resulting pull request back to the ticket. After the agent finishes, a reply in the Jira chat is sent as a follow-up; the agent continues on the same PR instead of opening a new one. The app pulls extra context from Confluence pages via MCP and supports custom agents, custom fields, and space-level guidance.
explainer.whyItMatters: Project managers and developers who live in Jira can now drive code changes without switching tabs, see the agent's progress, and steer it without losing the existing pull request. Cutting the back-and-forth between Jira and GitHub is the point of the launch.
explainer.forWho: Atlassian teams using GitHub Copilot
explainer.tryIt: Install GitHub Copilot for Jira from the Atlassian Marketplace

image.alt: GitHub Copilot for Jira streaming agent progress inside an Atlassian Jira ticket

quickFacts.label: New at GA
quickFacts.value: Streaming agent progress, post-session steering, simplified onboarding

quickFacts.label: From preview
quickFacts.value: Model selection, Confluence MCP context, custom agents, space-level guidance

faq.q: How do I install GitHub Copilot for Jira?
faq.a: GitHub Copilot for Jira is installed from the Atlassian Marketplace. The GA release simplified onboarding by reducing the configuration steps needed to connect a GitHub organization and its repositories to the Jira app, so an admin can wire it up without leaving Jira.

faq.q: What changed at general availability?
faq.a: GitHub Copilot for Jira at GA adds three things that were not in the preview: agent progress now streams live inside the Jira ticket, follow-up instructions in the Jira chat continue the same pull request instead of starting a new one, and the connection setup is shorter.

faq.q: Can the agent read Confluence pages?
faq.a: Yes. GitHub Copilot for Jira pulls extra context from Confluence pages through MCP, so the coding agent can reference linked design docs and runbooks while working on a Jira ticket without you copying the text into the prompt.

faq.q: Does it support custom agents and fields?
faq.a: GitHub Copilot for Jira supports custom agents, custom fields, model selection from Jira, Jira ticket references in pull request titles, space-level custom guidance, and review-request notifications in Jira — all of which carried over from the preview.

items.title: Wes Roth: 'OpenAI JUST announced JALAPENO'
items.summary: Wes Roth breaks down OpenAI's new Jalapeño inference chip, built with Broadcom, and what a custom OpenAI accelerator means for the AI hardware race.

items.title: OpenKnowledge v0.18.0 — local-first AI markdown wiki with codebase generator
items.summary: OpenKnowledge v0.18.0 ships a codebase-wiki starter pack that scaffolds an agent-written wiki of a repo, plus AI editing with Claude, Codex, and Cursor in a local-first markdown app.

explainer.tagline: Local-first AI markdown wiki — now scaffolds a codebase wiki an agent fills in.
explainer.whatIsIt: OpenKnowledge v0.18.0 is the latest stable release of Inkeep's local-first markdown editor and LLM wiki. The headline feature is the new `codebase-wiki` starter pack: it scaffolds a `wiki/` folder and an overview hub, then an agent fills it in with architecture notes, module descriptions, data flows, and Mermaid diagrams.
explainer.howItWorks: The starter pack writes a structured wiki layout into the repo, and a workflow-driven agent generates content into it using configurable parameters for audience and depth. Pages are version-controlled, private by default, and editable in OpenKnowledge's WYSIWYG markdown editor alongside Claude, Codex, and Cursor integrations and MCP-based agentic search.
explainer.whyItMatters: OpenKnowledge gives teams a way to keep a navigable, up-to-date knowledge base of a codebase without paying for a SaaS wiki. Because the files are plain markdown synced via Git, the same wiki can be read by humans and by other AI tools through MCP.
explainer.forWho: Engineering teams wanting a self-hosted AI wiki
