faq.a: The OpenAI Codex CLI was logging roughly 37 TB to a user's local SQLite store over 21 days of normal uptime. Extrapolated over a year that is about 640 TB of writes, which would exceed the rated endurance of a typical 1 TB consumer SSD many times over.

faq.q: What caused the SSD-fill bug in OpenAI Codex?
faq.a: Codex installed its SQLite feedback log with a default trace log level. That setting persisted every dependency target plus the raw WebSocket and SSE payloads from each model call, so the log file grew far faster than the agent's own activity would suggest.

faq.q: How did OpenAI fix the Codex logging bug?
faq.a: OpenAI merged two pull requests on June 22, 2026. PR #29432 stops Codex from logging every Responses WebSocket event, and PR #29457 filters out noisy log targets from the persistent store. Together they remove about 85% of the previous log volume.

faq.q: Do I need to update Codex CLI to avoid SSD damage?
faq.a: Yes. Users on the unpatched Codex CLI continue to write trace-level logs to SQLite at the same rate. Pulling the latest main branch and rebuilding picks up both fixes, after which the local log grows at a small fraction of the prior rate.

faq.q: Does the Codex bug affect ChatGPT or only the CLI?
faq.a: The bug is in the openai/codex command-line tool's local SQLite logging path, not ChatGPT. ChatGPT and other hosted OpenAI products do not write logs to your machine, so this disk-wear problem is specific to people running the Codex CLI on their own hardware.

image.alt: OpenAI Codex GitHub repository card

items.title: Two Minute Papers: 'DeepSeek Just Solved AI's Billion Dollar Problem'
items.summary: Two Minute Papers walks through the DualPath paper, which attacks the KV-cache I/O bottleneck behind agentic LLM serving costs and reports up to 1.96x higher online throughput.

explainer.tagline: Two Minute Papers explains why the storage bandwidth bottleneck — not raw compute — has been the real cost driver behind agentic LLM inference.
explainer.whatIsIt: A Two Minute Papers video covering DualPath, a research paper that targets the KV-cache loading bottleneck in disaggregated LLM serving. The video frames the result as a fix for the cost problem that has made running long-context agentic workloads so expensive at scale.
explainer.howItWorks: DualPath adds a second KV-cache loading route alongside the usual storage-to-prefill path: a storage-to-decode path that uses idle decode-engine NICs and then ships the cache to prefill engines over RDMA. Dynamic load balancing across the two paths keeps both sides busy instead of bandwidth-starving the prefill side.
explainer.whyItMatters: The paper reports up to 1.87x offline throughput and 1.96x average online throughput on production agentic workloads with no SLO violations. For anyone running long-running agents or multi-turn tools, that maps directly to lower per-token serving cost without new hardware.
explainer.forWho: ML infra engineers and teams running agentic LLM workloads

image.alt: Two Minute Papers thumbnail for 'DeepSeek Just Solved AI's Billion Dollar Problem'

items.title: Sakana Fugu — multi-agent orchestration model that matches Fable 5 on quality
items.summary: Sakana AI launched Fugu and Fugu Ultra, a multi-agent orchestration model delivered as one OpenAI-compatible API. Fugu Ultra coordinates a pool of expert agents and is reported to match Fable 5 on coding, reasoning, science, and agentic benchmarks.

explainer.tagline: Sakana AI ships Fugu, a single API that routes each request to a pool of frontier models and verifies the answer before returning it.
explainer.whatIsIt: Sakana Fugu is a multi-agent system delivered as one model. Behind a single OpenAI-compatible endpoint, Fugu plans a task, picks which models in its pool to call, splits work across them, verifies the partial results, and composes a final answer. Two variants ship at launch: Fugu for everyday low-latency work, and Fugu Ultra for high-stakes multi-step problems.
explainer.howItWorks: Two Sakana AI research lines feed Fugu. TRINITY (ICLR 2026) evolves an LLM coordinator that splits a task into Thinker, Worker, and Verifier roles. Conductor (ICLR 2026) trains a model with reinforcement learning to write natural-language coordination plans for a pool of LLMs. Fugu combines both: it acts as the orchestrator language model, calling other LLMs (and instances of itself) instead of merging weights.
explainer.whyItMatters: Fugu turns 'pick a model and a framework' into one API call, which lowers integration cost for teams building agents. Sakana AI says Fugu Ultra stands shoulder-to-shoulder with Anthropic's Fable 5 — relevant because Fable 5 is currently banned from US export, so an orchestration model that can route around any one supplier is structurally useful for enterprises affected by the ban.
explainer.forWho: Coding-agent and research-workflow builders who want one endpoint, not five

image.alt: Sakana Fugu launch artwork — fugu fish logo over orchestration diagram

quickFacts.label: Architecture
quickFacts.value: Multi-agent orchestration over a pool of LLMs

quickFacts.label: Availability
quickFacts.value: GA via console.sakana.ai; not in EU/EEA

quickFacts.label: Pricing
quickFacts.value: Subscription tiers plus pay-as-you-go for heavier use

faq.q: What is Sakana Fugu?
faq.a: Sakana Fugu is a multi-agent system that Sakana AI ships as one model behind a single API. When the user sends a prompt, Fugu plans the task, picks which models in its pool of frontier LLMs to call, runs them in parallel, verifies the partial results, and returns one combined answer.

faq.q: How does Fugu Ultra compare to Fable 5?
faq.a: Sakana AI says Fugu Ultra stands shoulder-to-shoulder with Anthropic's Fable 5 across coding, reasoning, science, and agentic benchmarks, including AutoResearch, Rubik's Cube solving, mechanical design, and financial time-series prediction. Sakana AI ran the comparison; independent benchmarks have not yet been published.

faq.q: How is Fugu Ultra different from Fugu?
faq.a: Fugu is the everyday model — tuned for low latency, meant for coding tools, chatbots, and interactive services. Fugu Ultra coordinates a deeper pool of expert agents and prioritizes answer quality on harder, longer, higher-stakes work like paper reproduction, cybersecurity analysis, and patent investigation.

faq.q: Where can I use Sakana Fugu?
faq.a: Sakana Fugu is generally available through the Sakana AI console at console.sakana.ai with an OpenAI-compatible API for Chat Completions and Responses. The service is not available in the EU/EEA at launch. Subscription tiers cover everyday use and a pay-as-you-go plan handles heavier or enterprise workloads.

faq.q: Can I exclude specific models from Fugu's pool?
faq.a: Yes. Fugu lets the caller exclude specific agents from the orchestration pool to meet compliance constraints, which matters for teams that cannot route requests through certain providers (e.g. Fable 5 under US export controls). The exclusion happens at the API layer rather than requiring a separate deployment.

items.title: Hermes Agent v0.17.0 — iMessage, WhatsApp, and async subagents from Nous
items.summary: Hermes Agent v0.17.0 'The Reach Release' adds iMessage support via Photon Spectrum (no Mac relay), an official WhatsApp Business Cloud adapter, Raft agent-network integration, and background subagents that return handles. 1,475 commits, 245 contributors.

faq.q: Does Hermes Agent v0.17.0 need a Mac relay for iMessage?
faq.a: Hermes Agent v0.17.0 sends and receives iMessage through the new Photon Spectrum bridge, which routes traffic service-side, so users no longer need to keep a Mac online to relay messages. The same release adds an official WhatsApp Business Cloud adapter alongside existing Telegram, Discord, Slack and Signal channels.

faq.q: How does v0.17.0 differ from v0.16.0?
faq.a: Hermes Agent v0.17.0 lands ~1,475 commits and ~800 merged PRs on top of v0.16.0, mainly adding iMessage and WhatsApp adapters, Raft agent-network integration, background subagents that return handles immediately, an image-editing mode in the image_generate tool, and Automation Blueprints that replace raw cron syntax for scheduling.

faq.q: What does it cost to run Hermes Agent?
faq.a: Hermes Agent is MIT-licensed open source from Nous Research, so the framework itself is free; users only pay the underlying LLM provider they connect (it supports 200+ models across providers without vendor lock-in). v0.17.0 lets Cursor's Composer model be called through an existing xAI Grok subscription.

faq.q: Is Hermes Agent v0.17.0 ready for self-hosting?
faq.a: Hermes Agent v0.17.0 ships as a single binary for macOS, Linux and Windows, with one-line installs from hermes-agent.nousresearch.com. The release adds a secure dashboard login, a redesigned Skills Hub with security scanning, atomic batch operations on the memory tool and live watch-windows for subagents, all aimed at long-running self-hosted deployments.

explainer.tagline: Nous Research's self-improving agent gains iMessage and WhatsApp adapters, Raft-network integration, and background subagents in 1,475 commits.
explainer.whatIsIt: Hermes Agent v0.17.0 'The Reach Release' adds iMessage support through the new Photon Spectrum bridge that does not need a Mac relay, an official WhatsApp Business Cloud channel, and integration with the Raft agent network. The release also ships background subagents that return handles immediately, an image-editing mode inside the image_generate tool, and a redesigned Skills Hub with security scanning.
explainer.howItWorks: Photon Spectrum routes iMessage through a service-side bridge, so the agent no longer depends on a Mac being online to relay messages. Background subagents now fork to a child process, return a handle to the parent, and report progress through a new watch-window panel in the desktop app. Automation Blueprints replace raw cron syntax with a graphical scheduler, and the model picker can call Cursor's Composer model through an existing xAI Grok subscription. The desktop app adds rebindable keyboard shortcuts, native OS notifications, custom themes pulled from the VS Code Marketplace, and per-thread composer drafts.
explainer.whyItMatters: Hermes is one of the most-installed open-source agent frameworks at roughly 199K stars, and locking iMessage and WhatsApp behind Mac relays or paid SaaS adapters was a long-standing rough edge. Shipping native channels for both, plus background subagents that do not block the foreground chat, brings the self-hostable stack closer to feature parity with hosted assistants on the platforms most users already live in.
explainer.forWho: self-hosted agent operators, OSS maintainers, indie developers

image.alt: GitHub social card for the Nous Research hermes-agent v0.17.0 release

items.title: Palmier Pro v0.3.5 — open-source macOS video editor for AI agents
items.summary: Palmier Pro v0.3.5 adds transcript-based cutting, ripple-insert trims with linked audio, folder imports, and a Claude Opus 4.8 upgrade to the Swift-native macOS video editor that exposes every timeline action to AI agents over MCP.

quickFacts.label: License
quickFacts.value: GPL-3.0 (editor); paid tier for generative AI

quickFacts.label: Agent integration
quickFacts.value: Claude Opus 4.8, Cursor, Codex via MCP

quickFacts.label: Pricing
quickFacts.value: Free editor; Pro AI tier from $29 / month

faq.q: How much does Palmier Pro cost?
faq.a: Palmier Pro is free and open source under GPL-3.0 — anyone can clone, build, and run the macOS editor without paying. Generative AI inside the timeline (Kling V3, Google Veo 3.1) and the bundled coding-agent integration require a paid Palmier account, with launch pricing starting at $29 per month for the Pro tier.

faq.q: Does Palmier Pro run on Windows or Linux?
faq.a: Palmier Pro is Swift-native and targets macOS Tahoe on Apple Silicon only. There is no Windows or Linux build of the v0.3.5 editor. The MCP server side is open code that could in principle be ported, but the timeline UI is tied to AppKit and Metal, so a cross-platform port is not on the announced roadmap.

faq.q: Which AI video models does Palmier Pro integrate with?
faq.a: Palmier Pro ships built-in generative support for Kling V3 and Google Veo 3.1, callable from the timeline as ordinary clips. Generated material can be mixed with user footage on the same multi-track timeline and exported as MP4 or NLE-XML for hand-off to Adobe Premiere Pro or DaVinci Resolve.

faq.q: How is Palmier Pro different from a regular video editor with AI plugins?
faq.a: Palmier Pro exposes every editor action — trim a clip, split a keyframe track, ripple-insert B-roll, transcript-cut a podcast — as a Model Context Protocol tool. Claude Opus 4.8, Cursor or Codex can then drive the whole timeline from chat, instead of running a one-shot AI generation and stopping at a single rendered asset.

faq.q: What is new in Palmier Pro v0.3.5?
faq.a: Palmier Pro v0.3.5 adds transcript-based cutting (highlight a phrase, the timeline ripples the matching footage with full undo), folder imports, a ripple-insert that respects linked audio, frame-accurate trim parameters and an upgrade to Claude Opus 4.8 for the agent integration. Three fatal Sentry crashes carried over from earlier 0.3 builds were also fixed.

explainer.tagline: Open-source macOS video editor that exposes every timeline action as an MCP tool for Claude, Cursor and Codex.
explainer.whatIsIt: v0.3.5 of Palmier Pro adds transcript-based cutting: highlight a word in the transcript and the timeline ripple-deletes the matching footage with a single undo. Palmier Pro itself is a Swift-native macOS video editor, GPL-3.0, that pairs a traditional multi-track timeline with built-in generative clips from Kling V3 and Google Veo 3.1.
explainer.howItWorks: Every editor action is exposed as a Model Context Protocol tool — split a keyframe track, ripple-insert with linked audio, fold in a folder of source files, trim by source frame number. A connected Claude Opus 4.8, Cursor or Codex session can then drive the whole edit from chat, while a human still scrubs the timeline directly in AppKit.
explainer.whyItMatters: Mainstream NLEs treat AI as a plugin that returns a finished asset; Palmier flips the relationship so the timeline IS the agent's environment. A Claude session can therefore build a podcast cut or a B-roll-laden explainer end-to-end and export NLE-XML for Premiere Pro or DaVinci Resolve. Three releases in three days (v0.3.3 → v0.3.5) show the team iterating in public.
explainer.forWho: Mac-based content creators and AI agent builders

image.alt: Palmier Pro GitHub repository — an open-source macOS video editor built for AI agents.

items.title: Cloudflare Temporary Accounts — AI agents deploy live Workers in seconds, no signup
items.summary: Cloudflare's new wrangler deploy --temporary command lets an AI agent provision a live Workers account in seconds without signup. The account stays usable for 60 minutes, after which a human can claim it permanently or let it auto-expire.

quickFacts.label: Claim flow
quickFacts.value: Human opens the claim URL, signs up, keeps deployment and resources

faq.q: How long does a Cloudflare temporary account last?
faq.a: A Cloudflare temporary account stays active for 60 minutes after wrangler deploy --temporary creates it. During that window the agent can redeploy, run the Worker, and verify the response. If no human claims the account by clicking the returned claim URL, Cloudflare deletes the account and every resource it created.

faq.q: How does a human turn a Cloudflare temporary account into a permanent one?
faq.a: Wrangler prints a claim URL alongside the deployment output. A human opens that URL inside the 60-minute window, signs up or logs into Cloudflare, and claims the temporary preview account. The deployed Worker and any bindings it created move into the new permanent account without redeploying.

faq.q: What Wrangler version do I need for Cloudflare temporary accounts?
faq.a: Cloudflare requires Wrangler 4.102.0 or newer for the --temporary flag. The CLI advertises the flag in its help output so agents can discover it the same way they discover other deploy options. Older Wrangler builds will simply not recognise --temporary and fall back to a normal authenticated deploy.

faq.q: How does Cloudflare's temporary-accounts feature compare to a regular Workers signup?
faq.a: A regular Cloudflare Workers signup requires a browser OAuth flow, email verification, and multi-factor setup before the first deploy. Cloudflare Temporary Accounts skip all three, which is what makes them workable for background AI agents that have no human in the loop at deploy time. Anything the agent ships still has to be claimed by a person to live past 60 minutes.

explainer.tagline: Cloudflare's wrangler deploy --temporary spins up a live Workers account for an AI agent in seconds, with no signup or browser OAuth.
explainer.whatIsIt: Cloudflare Temporary Accounts is a new deployment mode in Wrangler 4.102 that lets an AI agent ship a working Cloudflare Worker without first creating a Cloudflare account. The agent runs wrangler deploy --temporary, gets back an API token, a live Worker URL, and a claim link a human can use to keep the account.
explainer.howItWorks: When Wrangler sees --temporary, Cloudflare provisions a fresh preview account on the fly and returns credentials plus a 60-minute claim URL. The agent can iterate, redeploy, and curl the Worker during that window. If nobody claims the account in 60 minutes, Cloudflare deletes it along with every Worker and resource binding it produced. The auth.md protocol from WorkOS describes the discovery flow agents use, and a separate Stripe partnership covers paid-tier provisioning for accounts that get claimed.
explainer.whyItMatters: Most AI agent runs that build software get blocked at the deploy step because the target platform demands a browser-based signup. Temporary accounts unblock the loop: an agent can ship a working Worker, verify the output itself, and hand a live URL to a human who decides later whether to keep it. That removes one of the biggest friction points for autonomous coding agents on Cloudflare's developer platform.
explainer.forWho: developers building autonomous coding agents that target Cloudflare Workers

image.alt: Cloudflare blog header for Temporary Accounts for AI agents

items.title: Grok on Databricks — xAI models land in Agent Bricks via SpaceX deal
items.summary: Grok on Databricks makes Grok 4.3 and Grok Build 0.1 natively callable from Databricks Agent Bricks, so enterprise teams can wire xAI models into governed Lakehouse data without external pipelines.

quickFacts.label: Data path
quickFacts.value: Native Lakehouse context, no external pipeline

faq.q: Which Grok models are available on Databricks Agent Bricks?
faq.a: Grok on Databricks ships with two xAI models: Grok 4.3, the reasoning flagship with a one-million-token context window and a December 2025 knowledge cutoff, and Grok Build 0.1, the coding-specialised variant. Both run inside Agent Bricks alongside OpenAI, Anthropic, Gemini, Qwen, and Kimi options.

faq.q: How does Grok on Databricks handle enterprise data?
faq.a: Grok on Databricks connects directly to context stored in the Databricks Lakehouse. Agents reason over a company's structured and unstructured data inside Databricks' governance layer, so prompts and retrieval results stay in the customer's account rather than being routed through external SaaS pipelines.

faq.q: How much does Grok 4.3 cost when called through Databricks?
faq.a: On xAI's published API price list Grok 4.3 is $1.25 per million input tokens and $2.50 per million output tokens, and Grok Build 0.1 is $1.00 in / $2.00 out per million tokens. Databricks-tier pricing follows that schedule, with billing details surfaced in the Agent Bricks marketplace listing.

faq.q: Where else can teams run Grok besides Databricks?
faq.a: xAI's announcement points to Grok on Amazon Bedrock as the other governed cloud-platform path, alongside the direct xAI API. Databricks is the second major data-plus-AI platform to host Grok, after Bedrock added it earlier in the week, broadening enterprise access for SpaceXAI's frontier models.

explainer.tagline: xAI's Grok 4.3 and Grok Build 0.1 are now native model options inside Databricks' Agent Bricks platform.
explainer.whatIsIt: Grok on Databricks adds two xAI models — Grok 4.3 reasoning and Grok Build 0.1 coding — as native options inside Databricks Agent Bricks, Databricks' developer platform for production agents. The integration was announced at the Databricks 2026 Data + AI Summit through a partnership with SpaceXAI. Agent Bricks teams can now pick Grok from the same menu as OpenAI, Anthropic, Gemini, Qwen, and Kimi.
explainer.howItWorks: Inside Agent Bricks, Grok models read context directly from Databricks' Lakehouse rather than from an external retrieval service, so structured tables and unstructured documents stay in the customer's governed environment. xAI says agents can reason over large volumes of enterprise data without routing it through outside pipelines, and notes that model partners do not retain data submitted through these features.
explainer.whyItMatters: For Databricks customers this is the first time xAI's frontier models are a one-click choice next to OpenAI, Anthropic, and Google in a governed enterprise platform. It matters because most regulated organisations cannot move data into a vendor's hosted endpoint, and Grok on Databricks lets them try Grok 4.3's one-million-token context against their own Lakehouse tables without that hurdle.
explainer.forWho: enterprise engineering teams already building on Databricks Agent Bricks

image.alt: xAI announcement card for Grok models on Databricks Agent Bricks

items.title: John Jumper to Anthropic — Nobel laureate AlphaFold creator leaves DeepMind
items.summary: John Jumper, the 2024 Nobel chemistry laureate and AlphaFold co-creator, posted on X that he is leaving Google DeepMind after nearly nine years to join Anthropic. He is the second senior Google AI departure this week after Noam Shazeer.

quickFacts.label: Transition
quickFacts.value: Stays at DeepMind through end of 2026

faq.q: What did John Jumper say in his announcement?
faq.a: John Jumper posted on X on June 19, 2026 that he is leaving Google DeepMind after nearly nine years to join Anthropic, and that he plans to take time to recharge before starting his new role. He did not give a specific start date or describe what he will work on at Anthropic.

faq.q: What will John Jumper do at Anthropic?
faq.a: Anthropic has not yet disclosed John Jumper's role. Anthropic does not currently ship a protein-folding model, so observers expect his focus to broaden beyond AlphaFold-style biology and into the foundational research that underpins Claude. The lab has separately said Jumper will stay at Google DeepMind through the end of 2026 to help with the transition.

faq.q: How does this compare to Noam Shazeer's move to OpenAI?
faq.a: John Jumper's announcement comes two days after Noam Shazeer, Google's Gemini co-lead and 'Attention Is All You Need' co-author, said he is joining OpenAI as Lead for Architecture Research. Both departures take senior Google research leaders to direct frontier-lab rivals in a single week, intensifying the 2026 AI talent war.

faq.q: Why does this matter for Google DeepMind?
faq.a: John Jumper led the AlphaFold work that won Demis Hassabis and Jumper the 2024 Nobel Prize in Chemistry, and that anchors DeepMind's science-AI reputation. Losing him along with Noam Shazeer in the same week removes two of the lab's most visible technical leaders during a period when Google is defending Gemini's market share against Anthropic and OpenAI.

explainer.tagline: Nobel chemistry laureate and AlphaFold co-creator John Jumper is leaving Google DeepMind after nine years to join Anthropic.
explainer.whatIsIt: John Jumper announced on X on June 19, 2026 that he is leaving Google DeepMind, where he has been a vice president and Engineering Fellow for nearly nine years, to join Anthropic. Jumper shared the 2024 Nobel Prize in Chemistry with Demis Hassabis for AlphaFold, the deep-learning system that predicted the structure of more than 200 million proteins.
explainer.howItWorks: Anthropic has not disclosed Jumper's title or focus area, and Jumper said on X he will take a break before starting. Google DeepMind told reporters he will stay through the end of 2026 to help hand off ongoing work. Anthropic does not currently ship a protein-folding product, so observers expect Jumper's remit to be broader than the AlphaFold line he led at DeepMind.
explainer.whyItMatters: Two of Google's most senior AI researchers have left for direct rivals in three days: Noam Shazeer to OpenAI on June 17, and now John Jumper to Anthropic. The Jumper move strips DeepMind of the public face of its Nobel-winning science-AI program and pulls that credibility toward Anthropic ahead of OpenAI's expected IPO. For researchers, it signals that the most consequential biology-AI work may now happen outside Google.
explainer.forWho: researchers and labs tracking where Nobel-tier AI leaders work next

image.alt: John Jumper headshot — Nobel-winning AlphaFold co-creator departing Google DeepMind for Anthropic

items.title: Nathan Lambert: 'Banning Open Source AI Would Be A Mistake'
items.summary: Nathan Lambert and Kevin Xu argue banning open-source AI would hurt US security, education, and competition. Their Interconnects post responds to an executive order to review AI models and a block on foreign access to Anthropic models.

image.alt: Cover image for Nathan Lambert's Interconnects post 'Banning open source AI would be a mistake'

explainer.tagline: Nathan Lambert and Kevin Xu argue open-source AI is a US asset, not a security risk to ban.
explainer.whatIsIt: Nathan Lambert and Kevin Xu publish a defense of open-source AI on Interconnects. The post responds to a US executive order ordering a review of AI models, a congressional proposal to legislate AI further, and a recent action that blocks foreign nationals from accessing Anthropic's advanced models. They argue restricting open weights would harm American interests.
explainer.howItWorks: The piece lays out four reasons restriction backfires. Open source is how students around the world learn to build software at all. Open weights let small teams compete with frontier labs and keep monopolies in check. Public code is safer because more researchers can audit it. And open source has produced more than $8 trillion in worldwide economic value historically.
explainer.whyItMatters: American policymakers face real questions about access to Chinese open models like GLM-5.2 and Qwen. Lambert and Xu push back on framing restriction as the answer. They want more federal support for domestic open work, framing transparency as aligned with US values of competition and innovation.

items.title: Moebius — 0.22B image inpainting matches FLUX.1-Fill-Dev's 11.9B
items.summary: Moebius is a 0.22B image inpainting model from HUST and VIVO AI Lab that matches the 11.9B FLUX.1-Fill-Dev across six benchmarks while running over 15x faster, with code and weights now on GitHub.

explainer.tagline: A 226M-parameter inpainting model that keeps up with 11.9B systems and runs 15x faster.
explainer.whatIsIt: Moebius is a lightweight image inpainting framework from HUST and VIVO AI Lab. The 0.22B model is under 2% the size of FLUX.1-Fill-Dev (11.9B) and matches or beats it on six benchmarks across Places2, CelebA-HQ, and FFHQ. Moebius's training and inference code plus model weights shipped on June 18, 2026.
explainer.howItWorks: Moebius introduces a Local-lambda Mix Interaction block to fight the representation bottleneck of extreme compression, paired with adaptive multi-granularity distillation that transfers knowledge from a 10B-class teacher. The architecture targets local context at multiple scales, which lets a tiny network preserve detail in masked regions. Per-step inference runs in 26.01 ms.
explainer.whyItMatters: Moebius shows that high-quality inpainting does not need a billion-parameter generator. Teams that could not afford to deploy FLUX.1-Fill-Dev now have an Apache-2.0 model 50x smaller that runs on commodity GPUs. The 15x speedup also opens up interactive editing flows where each user click triggers a new inpaint.
explainer.forWho: ML researchers building image editing tools, product teams adding inpainting to apps, students studying model distillation.

image.alt: Hugging Face paper thumbnail for Moebius image inpainting framework

items.title: agent-eval — Hugging Face harness benchmarks coding agents on your own library
items.summary: Hugging Face shipped agent-eval, an open harness that measures how well coding agents like Kimi-K2.6 and GLM-5.1 use a library — not just task completion, but token cost, time, and error rate across bare, clone, and skill access tiers.

explainer.tagline: Hugging Face's agent-eval scores libraries on whether agents can actually use them, not just whether they succeed.
explainer.whatIsIt: agent-eval is an open evaluation harness from Hugging Face for testing how well open coding agents work with a specific library. The blog post argues that 'if it isn't tested, then it doesn't work' should apply to agent usability, not just human usability. Released June 18, 2026 by Lysandre, Nathan Habib, Pedro Cuenca and nine other contributors.
explainer.howItWorks: agent-eval runs each candidate model against the target library at three access tiers: 'bare' (raw API), 'clone' (the library copied into context), and 'skill' (a curated skill bundle). For every run the harness records token consumption, wall-clock time, error rates, and behavioural markers like retry loops — not just pass/fail. Reported runs cover Kimi-K2.6, GLM-5.1, MiniMax-M2.7, Qwen3-4B and Qwen3-14B.
explainer.whyItMatters: Library maintainers can finally answer 'is our API agent-friendly?' with numbers. agent-eval surfaces where docs are missing or APIs are too clever for agents to invoke, which is the bottleneck for getting Claude Code, Cursor, and other agents to use a stack reliably. The harness is open so anyone can plug in their own library.
explainer.forWho: Library maintainers, agent toolers, ML engineers benchmarking small open models.

image.alt: agent-eval harness thumbnail from Hugging Face

items.title: Beyond LoRA — Hugging Face benchmark shows OFT and BEFT can beat the default
items.summary: Hugging Face benchmarked five PEFT methods on identical tasks and found OFT beats LoRA on image fine-tuning while BEFT and Lily trade memory for accuracy on math. PEFT also gained an adapter-to-LoRA converter for vLLM compatibility.

explainer.tagline: OFT, BEFT, and Lily can beat LoRA — and PEFT now converts them all to LoRA format for vLLM.
explainer.whatIsIt: 'Beyond LoRA' is a Hugging Face benchmark study, published June 18, 2026, comparing parameter-efficient fine-tuning methods head-to-head. The post pairs the comparison with a new PEFT library feature: any supported adapter can be converted to a LoRA on save, which lets downstream packages like vLLM load it without special handling. Authors: Benjamin Bossan, Sayak Paul, Marian Dosmann, and Kashif Rasul.
explainer.howItWorks: The Beyond LoRA benchmark runs each method under matched compute and rank on two tasks: MetaMathQA fine-tuning for language models and a cat-plushy concept-learning task for image generation. Beyond LoRA reports accuracy, memory, and training time per method, surfacing tradeoffs that single-method papers rarely show side by side. The PEFT release adds `convert_to_lora()` so non-LoRA adapters can serve through any LoRA-aware runtime.
explainer.whyItMatters: LoRA is on 98.4% of PEFT model cards, but Beyond LoRA shows that default is not always the best — OFT wins on image tasks and BEFT or Lily can win on math when memory is tight. The conversion feature removes the deployment penalty that previously locked teams into LoRA, opening up real method choice for the first time.
explainer.forWho: ML practitioners fine-tuning open models, infra teams running vLLM, researchers comparing PEFT methods.

image.alt: Beyond LoRA benchmark thumbnail comparing PEFT methods on Hugging Face

items.title: Cursor 3.8 — /automate skill plus new GitHub and Slack automation triggers
items.summary: Cursor 3.8 launches Cursor Automations: describe a task in plain English with /automate and it runs on its own when a GitHub event or Slack emoji react fires. Adds five GitHub triggers; cloud agents get computer use by default.

image.alt: Cursor 3.8 changelog cover graphic for the Improvements to Cursor Automations release

explainer.tagline: Describe a task in plain English and Cursor 3.8 turns it into an automation that runs itself when a GitHub event or a Slack emoji fires.
explainer.whatIsIt: Cursor 3.8 adds Cursor Automations: saved agent workflows that fire on their own when an outside event happens, instead of waiting for you to type a prompt. You build one with the new /automate skill — describe the job in plain language and Cursor writes the trigger, instructions, and tool list for you, all inside a normal local agent session.
explainer.howItWorks: When a trigger fires, the automation hands the job to a cloud agent that works from the instructions you captured at setup, so nobody has to be at the keyboard. Those cloud agents now have the computer-use tool on by default, which lets them drive a browser or app and hand back a short demo or screenshots as proof of the work. A watched event can be anything from a GitHub review comment to a Slack reaction, and a finished automation can open its own pull request when it is done.
explainer.whyItMatters: A Cursor agent used to wait for you to prompt it; an Automation starts from a teammate's signal instead — a review comment, a green CI run, a Slack emoji — so the work begins the moment it is needed rather than when someone remembers to ask. Because /automate builds the whole thing from one sentence, there is no YAML or glue code to write, so people who have never scripted a CI job can still stand up real event-driven agents. The bundled starter templates make the point: one triages a failed GitHub Actions run, another fixes pull-request review comments, both running unattended.
explainer.forWho: teams running cloud-based coding agents on top of GitHub and Slack
explainer.tryIt: Update Cursor, then run /automate in any agent chat

quickFacts.label: Set up with
quickFacts.value: /automate skill — describe the task in plain English

quickFacts.label: GitHub triggers
quickFacts.value: 5 — issue comment, PR review comment, review submitted, review thread resolved/unresolved, workflow run completed

quickFacts.label: Slack trigger
quickFacts.value: React to a message with a designated emoji

quickFacts.label: Cloud agents
quickFacts.value: Computer-use tool on by default — produce demos + screenshots

quickFacts.label: Starter templates
quickFacts.value: Triage failed GitHub Actions; auto-fix PR review comments

quickFacts.label: Also new
quickFacts.value: Save incomplete automations, auto-open PRs, delete memory files in the UI

faq.q: Do Cursor Automations run locally or in the cloud?
faq.a: Cursor Automations are set up inside a normal local Cursor 3.8 agent session, but they run in the cloud. When the trigger fires, Cursor spawns a cloud agent that carries out the saved instructions on its own, so your machine does not need to be open for the automation to run.

faq.q: What can a Cursor 3.8 automation do on its own?
faq.a: A Cursor 3.8 automation runs a full coding agent unattended. The bundled starter templates, for example, triage a failed GitHub Actions run and auto-fix pull-request review comments. An automation can also open its own pull request and, with computer use on by default, hand back a demo or screenshots of the result.

faq.q: Can you save a Cursor automation before it is finished?
faq.a: Yes. Cursor 3.8 lets you save an automation while it is still incomplete and come back later — for instance, to set up MCP authentication — without losing your progress. You can also delete memory files from the UI, or tell the automation to delete its own outdated memories.

faq.q: How are Cursor Automations different from GitHub Actions?
faq.a: A GitHub Actions workflow is a YAML script; a Cursor 3.8 automation is a coding agent you describe in one plain-English sentence with /automate. It can read and edit your code, open a pull request, and use a computer to make demos, and it can fire on signals a YAML workflow cannot watch directly, like a Slack emoji reaction or a resolved review thread.

items.title: Noam Shazeer to OpenAI — Gemini co-lead becomes Lead for Architecture Research
items.summary: Noam Shazeer, Google VP and Gemini co-lead, is leaving Google to join OpenAI as Lead for Architecture Research, two years after Google paid $2.7B to bring him back from Character.AI.

quickFacts.label: Who
quickFacts.value: Noam Shazeer, co-author of 'Attention Is All You Need'

quickFacts.label: Context
quickFacts.value: Google paid $2.7B in 2024 to bring him back from Character.AI

faq.q: What is Noam Shazeer's new role at OpenAI?
faq.a: Noam Shazeer is joining OpenAI as Lead for Architecture Research, a role confirmed by OpenAI Chief Research Officer Mark Chen. In that position Shazeer will oversee work on the fundamental design of OpenAI's next generation of advanced AI models, including the model architecture choices that shape capability, cost, and scaling behavior.

faq.q: Why did Noam Shazeer leave Google?
faq.a: Noam Shazeer left Google after two years as a co-lead of Gemini, having previously joined Google in 2000 and then founded Character.AI in 2021. Google brought him back in 2024 in a roughly $2.7 billion deal. He has not publicly stated the reason; the move comes amid an intense AI talent war and ahead of OpenAI's expected IPO.

faq.q: What did Sam Altman say about hiring Noam Shazeer?
faq.a: Sam Altman posted on X that 'noam is one of the people I have most wanted to work with since the very beginning of openai.' Shazeer himself posted that he was 'excited to share that I'll be joining OpenAI and look forward to working with the exceptional team there.' Google said it was 'grateful for Noam's meaningful contributions to Google over the years.'

faq.q: What is Noam Shazeer famous for?
faq.a: Noam Shazeer is best known as a lead author of the 2017 paper 'Attention Is All You Need,' which introduced the Transformer architecture that underpins ChatGPT, Gemini, Claude, and almost every modern LLM. He also co-founded Character.AI and co-led Google's Gemini program. In 2026 he was elected to the U.S. National Academy of Engineering.

explainer.tagline: Noam Shazeer, co-author of 'Attention Is All You Need' and Gemini co-lead, is moving from Google to OpenAI to head architecture research.
explainer.whatIsIt: Noam Shazeer is one of the most consequential AI engineers alive — a lead author of the 2017 Transformer paper, founder of Character.AI, and since 2024 a vice president of engineering and co-lead of Google's Gemini models. On June 17, 2026 he announced on X that he is leaving Google to join OpenAI.
explainer.howItWorks: OpenAI Chief Research Officer Mark Chen confirmed Shazeer's title as 'Lead for Architecture Research,' where Shazeer will oversee the fundamental design of OpenAI's next-generation models. Google brought Shazeer back from Character.AI in 2024 in a roughly $2.7 billion deal that also pulled in his research team. Sam Altman posted that 'noam is one of the people I have most wanted to work with since the very beginning of openai.'
explainer.whyItMatters: Shazeer's architectural choices — Transformer, mixture-of-experts, multi-query attention — have shaped what frontier models look like for the last decade. Moving him from Gemini to OpenAI shifts one of the few people who can redesign a flagship model architecture across the lab line, ahead of OpenAI's expected IPO. For developers, the move signals that architecture, not just scale or RLHF, is the next axis of competition.
explainer.forWho: anyone tracking who shapes the next generation of frontier models

image.alt: Noam Shazeer headshot — Gemini co-lead departing Google for OpenAI

items.title: In the Weights — ex-OpenAI tool scores whether AI models remember your name
items.summary: Joey Flynn and Thomas Dimson, both ex-OpenAI, launched a free site that types a name into multiple LLMs in parallel and returns a 0–996 'strength score' for how well models recall the person from training data alone.

quickFacts.label: What it does
quickFacts.value: Probes whether a person appears in LLM training data

quickFacts.label: Method
quickFacts.value: Parallel queries across frontier and small models, clustered

faq.q: How does In the Weights work?
faq.a: In the Weights takes a name, queries several large language models in parallel without web search, clusters their responses to identify which person each model is describing, and outputs a strength score from 0 to 996. Higher scores mean a model can reliably recall the person from training data alone; the top score of 996 is reserved for figures like Mozart, Shakespeare, and Taylor Swift.

faq.q: Who built In the Weights?
faq.a: In the Weights was built by Joey Flynn and Thomas Dimson, two former OpenAI employees. They posted it as a Show HN on June 18, 2026, where it reached 430 points and 236 comments. The site frames itself as a way to make 'training data memorization' tangible for anyone who wants to check whether they appear in modern LLM weights.

faq.q: Is In the Weights accurate?
faq.a: In the Weights is a probe, not a verifier — the creators acknowledge models fabricate biographical details, typos hurt scores, and common names confuse disambiguation. Smaller models hallucinate more than frontier models. Users in the HN thread report it scores academics with papers and open-source contributors reasonably well but invents biographies for unfamiliar names, especially athletes.

faq.q: What can I learn from In the Weights?
faq.a: In the Weights shows which LLMs encode you into their weights and at what strength, exposing how rare names tip into confabulation and how memorization scales with model size. Researchers can use it to study training-data inclusion, writers can check whether models will recognize a quote attribution, and developers can see what an LLM can answer about a person before any retrieval.

explainer.tagline: In the Weights queries multiple LLMs in parallel and scores how strongly each model remembers a person you name.
explainer.whatIsIt: In the Weights is a free web tool built by Joey Flynn and Thomas Dimson, both former OpenAI engineers. You type a person's name and the site reports whether — and how strongly — major language models can recall that person from training data alone, without any web search.
explainer.howItWorks: In the Weights sends the name to several frontier and smaller models in parallel, clusters the responses so it can tell which person each model is describing, and combines the results into a single 'strength score' from 0 to 996. The maximum is reserved for names like Mozart, Shakespeare, and Taylor Swift. The creators note that smaller models hallucinate more and that common names confuse disambiguation.
explainer.whyItMatters: In the Weights makes 'training data memorization' tangible. Researchers can probe what LLMs encode about real people, writers can check what a model will recall before relying on it for biographies, and developers can see how much grounding their app needs before a retrieval layer is required. The Show HN hit 430 points in a day, signaling sustained interest in the question of who is — and isn't — in the weights.
explainer.forWho: AI researchers, writers, and developers curious about LLM memorization

image.alt: 'In the Weights' homepage banner — query LLMs to see if they recall a name

items.title: DeepMind AI Control Roadmap — defense-in-depth for misaligned AI agents
items.summary: Google DeepMind publishes the AI Control Roadmap: a defense-in-depth framework that treats internal AI agents as insider threats, with trusted supervisor models monitoring their actions in real time.

quickFacts.label: Authors
quickFacts.value: Rohin Shah, Four Flynn, Mary Phuong, Erik Jenner, Laurent Simon, Lewis Ho, Sebastian Farquhar, Scott Coull

faq.q: What is the DeepMind AI Control Roadmap?
faq.a: The AI Control Roadmap is a Google DeepMind framework for managing AI agents the lab cannot yet fully trust. DeepMind treats capable internal agents as potential insider threats and uses trusted supervisor AI systems to monitor their reasoning, detect harmful actions, and block them in real time before they execute.

faq.q: What does the framework actually cover?
faq.a: The AI Control Roadmap covers three areas: threat modeling using MITRE ATT&CK adapted for AI, mitigation deployment via detection and prevention (supervisor monitoring and synchronous blocking on high-risk actions), and performance metrics for coverage, recall, and response time.

faq.q: What did DeepMind learn from one million coding agent trajectories?
faq.a: From around one million coding agent trajectories, DeepMind reports that most flagged behaviors come from agent misinterpretation or overeagerness rather than adversarial intent. That evidence informs how the AI Control Roadmap maps protocols to capability milestones across detection evasion and harm potential.

faq.q: What is the Three Layers of Agent Security paper?
faq.a: Three Layers of Agent Security is a companion DeepMind PDF written for policymakers. It frames agent security at three levels: individual agents, multi-agent systems, and ecosystem resilience. The paper explains the same defense-in-depth ideas as the AI Control Roadmap without the internal-engineering detail.

faq.q: Why does this matter for the wider AI industry?
faq.a: The AI Control Roadmap shifts agent safety from alignment-only to system-level security, similar to zero-trust cybersecurity. If trusted supervisor models can block harmful actions even when the underlying agent is imperfectly aligned, other labs and enterprises deploying agents can adopt a similar layered playbook.

explainer.tagline: Google DeepMind treats internal AI agents as insider threats and uses supervisor AI to block harmful actions in real time.
explainer.whatIsIt: The AI Control Roadmap is a defense-in-depth security framework Google DeepMind built for its own internal AI agents. The roadmap assumes capable agents may be imperfectly aligned and that alignment alone is not enough — so it adds a system-level security layer around them. A companion policy paper, Three Layers of Agent Security, generalizes the same ideas for outside policymakers.
explainer.howItWorks: The AI Control Roadmap has three parts. First, threat modeling adapted from MITRE ATT&CK, mapped to AI capability milestones across detection evasion and harm potential. Second, mitigation deployment: a trusted supervisor AI watches agent reasoning and either flags actions asynchronously for human review or blocks the riskiest ones synchronously before they run. Third, measurement: coverage, recall, and response-time metrics, calibrated against an analysis of about one million internal coding agent trajectories.
explainer.whyItMatters: The AI Control Roadmap is one of the first concrete playbooks for living with imperfectly aligned, capable AI agents inside a frontier lab. By framing the problem as zero-trust insider security rather than only alignment, DeepMind makes it easier for other labs and enterprises to copy the layered approach as they deploy real agents into production codebases and tools.
explainer.forWho: AI safety teams, security engineers, and policymakers working on agent deployment.
explainer.tryIt: Read the AI Control Roadmap PDF linked from the post.

image.alt: Google DeepMind blog hero for Securing the future of AI agents.

links.label: Three Layers of Agent Security PDF

items.title: Two Minute Papers: 'Scientists Found A Better Language For AI Agents'
items.summary: Two Minute Papers covers RecursiveMAS, a UIUC/Stanford/NVIDIA/MIT framework that lets multi-agent systems trade latent thoughts instead of text and reports 2.4x faster inference with 75.6% fewer tokens.

explainer.tagline: Two Minute Papers walks through RecursiveMAS, a multi-agent framework that swaps text messages for shared latent thoughts.
explainer.whatIsIt: A Two Minute Papers video from Karoly Zsolnai-Feher explaining RecursiveMAS, a multi-agent system from UIUC, Stanford, NVIDIA, and MIT. RecursiveMAS connects specialist LLM agents through a small RecursiveLink module so they exchange compressed latent states across reasoning rounds instead of long text turns.
explainer.howItWorks: RecursiveMAS treats the whole agent team as one recursive computation. Each agent reads and writes shared latent thoughts through RecursiveLink, supports four patterns (sequential, mixture, distillation, deliberation), and only trains about 0.31% of the system's parameters while the rest of the agents co-evolve.
explainer.whyItMatters: On the reported benchmarks RecursiveMAS lifts accuracy by 8.3 points on average, runs 2.4x faster end to end, and cuts token use by 75.6% at the deepest recursion depth, directly attacking the cost and latency that have kept production multi-agent stacks small.
explainer.forWho: Builders of multi-agent and tool-using LLM systems

image.alt: Two Minute Papers thumbnail for 'Scientists Found A Better Language For AI Agents'

items.title: MolmoMotion — Ai2's language-guided 3D motion forecasting models
items.summary: Ai2 released MolmoMotion, two open models that predict where points on objects will move in 3D space from a video frame plus a text instruction. The drop bundles a 1.16M-video training set and the PointMotionBench eval, and lifts a robot pick-and-place baseline from 56.0% to 76.3%.

quickFacts.label: Input
quickFacts.value: Video frame + marked points + text instruction

quickFacts.label: Output
quickFacts.value: 3D point trajectories over the next few seconds

explainer.tagline: MolmoMotion predicts how points on objects move in 3D from a video frame and a text instruction, with weights, a 1.16M-video dataset, and a benchmark.
explainer.whatIsIt: MolmoMotion is a pair of open motion-forecasting models from Ai2. You give MolmoMotion a single video frame, a few marked points on objects, and a text instruction like 'pour the water into the cup,' and MolmoMotion predicts where those points travel through 3D space over the next few seconds.
explainer.howItWorks: MolmoMotion ships in two variants. MolmoMotion-AR is autoregressive — it writes future 3D coordinates as structured text, step by step, on top of an Ai2 vision-language backbone. MolmoMotion-FM uses flow matching — it learns to transform random noise into a full trajectory in continuous 3D space, which captures uncertainty better when multiple futures are plausible. Both are trained on MolmoMotion-1M, a new 1.16M-video corpus where every clip carries action-described, object-grounded 3D point trajectories spanning 736 motion types across 5.6K distinct objects.
explainer.whyItMatters: Motion forecasting is the bridge between perception and action — for both robots and video generators. MolmoMotion outperforms every existing 3D motion forecaster on the new PointMotionBench eval and lifts a robot pick-and-place success rate from 56.0% to 76.3%. Ai2 also released MolmoMotion-1M and PointMotionBench, so other labs can train and compare without rebuilding the corpus.
explainer.forWho: Robotics researchers, video-generation researchers, multimodal ML practitioners

image.alt: MolmoMotion announcement banner showing 3D point trajectories overlaid on a video frame

faq.q: What is MolmoMotion?
faq.a: MolmoMotion is a pair of open models from the Allen Institute for AI, released on June 17, 2026, that predict 3D point trajectories on objects from a video frame, a few marked points, and a natural-language instruction. MolmoMotion ships in two variants — MolmoMotion-AR (autoregressive) and MolmoMotion-FM (flow-matching) — together with the 1.16M-video MolmoMotion-1M training set and the PointMotionBench evaluation.

faq.q: How well does MolmoMotion perform on robots?
faq.a: MolmoMotion completes 76.3% of pick-and-place tasks in robotics evaluations, versus 56.0% for the prior baseline reported in the announcement. On the dedicated PointMotionBench eval, MolmoMotion outperforms all existing 3D motion-forecasting methods, according to the Ai2 release.

faq.q: What is the difference between MolmoMotion-AR and MolmoMotion-FM?
faq.a: MolmoMotion-AR predicts future coordinates step by step and represents 3D coordinates as structured text. MolmoMotion-FM predicts trajectories in continuous 3D space by transforming noise into motion through flow matching, which Ai2 says is better suited for representing uncertainty when multiple plausible futures exist.

items.title: MosaicLeaks — ServiceNow benchmark for research-agent privacy leaks
items.summary: ServiceNow released MosaicLeaks, a 1,001-chain benchmark that measures how much private context a research agent leaks into its web queries, plus PA-DR, an RL recipe that drops leakage from 51.7% to 9.9% on Qwen3-4B with no loss of task success.

quickFacts.label: Benchmark size
quickFacts.value: 1,001 multi-hop research chains (559 train / 98 val / 344 test)

quickFacts.label: Threat model
quickFacts.value: Private enterprise docs + controlled public web corpus

explainer.tagline: MosaicLeaks measures how much an agent leaks into its web searches; PA-DR's privacy-aware RL cuts leakage from 51.7% to 9.9% on Qwen3-4B with no loss of task success.
explainer.whatIsIt: MosaicLeaks is a benchmark from ServiceNow Research that measures privacy leakage by deep-research agents. Each of the 1,001 chains forces an agent to interleave searches over a private enterprise document set with searches against a controlled public web corpus, so leakage shows up directly in the queries the agent sends out.
explainer.howItWorks: MosaicLeaks builds each chain as a multi-hop research task with bridging entities tying the private and public sides together. The split is 559 training, 98 validation, and 344 held-out-company test chains. The companion PA-DR (Privacy-Aware Deep Research) training recipe combines situational task rewards with a learned privacy reward — judging whether the current query leaks private content directly and whether adding it to the existing query log creates a new 'mosaic' leak. The released paper reports 5-6x sample-efficiency gains from the situational rewards.
explainer.whyItMatters: Research agents are starting to land inside enterprises, and 'the agent typed my customer list into Google' is the failure mode security teams fear. MosaicLeaks gives the field a shared scoreboard, and PA-DR shows the leakage rate is fixable — on Qwen3-4B, leakage drops from 51.7% to 9.9% while task success barely moves (58.7% vs 59.3%). Teams shipping deep-research agents can adopt PA-DR or use MosaicLeaks to red-team their own pipeline.
explainer.forWho: Agent builders shipping to regulated enterprises, safety researchers, RLHF teams

image.alt: MosaicLeaks announcement card from ServiceNow Research

items.title: Agentic Resource Discovery — HF, Microsoft, Google open spec for tool lookup
items.summary: Hugging Face, Microsoft, Google, and GoDaddy launched Agentic Resource Discovery, an open spec that lets agents find MCP servers, skills, and A2A endpoints at runtime. ARD defines an ai-catalog.json manifest and a POST /search registry API, with a reference Discover Tool on HF.

quickFacts.label: Relationship to MCP
quickFacts.value: Layer above — finds the tools MCP then calls

explainer.tagline: Agentic Resource Discovery is an open spec from HF, Microsoft, Google and GoDaddy that lets agents find MCP servers, skills, and A2A endpoints at runtime.
explainer.whatIsIt: Agentic Resource Discovery (ARD) is an open specification, launched as a draft, that defines how agents discover the tools and skills available to them at runtime — instead of being shipped with a hard-coded list of MCP servers or installed plugins. Hugging Face, Microsoft, Google, and GoDaddy co-developed ARD, and Hugging Face shipped the first reference implementation.
explainer.howItWorks: ARD has two layers. The first is a Static Manifest Format: a publisher hosts an ai-catalog.json file at a well-known URL (for example huggingface.co/.well-known/ai-catalog.json) that lists the MCP servers, skills, ML apps, and A2A endpoints they expose. The second is a Dynamic Registry API — a POST /search endpoint that takes an intent and returns ranked, federated results across many catalogs. ARD sits above MCP rather than replacing it: MCP defines how an agent CALLS a tool, ARD defines how an agent FINDS the tool first. Hugging Face's hf-discover demo serves the HF catalog through huggingface-hf-discover.hf.space.
explainer.whyItMatters: Right now every coding agent ships with a static MCP server list, and adding a new tool means a config edit. ARD reframes tool access as a search problem — an agent gets a fresh intent ('fine-tune a small image model'), queries an ARD registry, and gets back the relevant MCP server, skill, or A2A endpoint without anyone editing settings. With HF, Microsoft, and Google co-signing, ARD is the first credible attempt to make agent capabilities composable across vendors.
explainer.forWho: Agent platform builders, MCP server maintainers, anyone publishing AI tools

image.alt: Agentic Resource Discovery announcement banner

faq.q: What is Agentic Resource Discovery?
faq.a: Agentic Resource Discovery (ARD) is an open draft specification, launched June 17, 2026 by Hugging Face, Microsoft, Google, and GoDaddy, that lets AI agents find available tools, skills, MCP servers, and A2A endpoints at runtime. ARD defines a static ai-catalog.json manifest hosted at a well-known URL plus a dynamic POST /search registry API for federated, ranked discovery.

faq.q: How does ARD relate to MCP?
faq.a: Agentic Resource Discovery complements MCP rather than competing with it. MCP defines how an agent calls a tool once it knows about it; ARD defines how the agent finds the tool in the first place. An agent uses ARD's POST /search to discover candidate MCP servers for an intent, then connects to those servers through the standard MCP protocol.

faq.q: Who can publish an ARD catalog?
faq.a: Any organization can publish an Agentic Resource Discovery catalog by hosting an ai-catalog.json file at /.well-known/ai-catalog.json on their domain. Hugging Face has published the first catalog at huggingface.co/.well-known/ai-catalog.json, exposing semantic search over Skills, ML apps, and MCP servers via the open-source huggingface/hf-discover reference implementation.

items.title: Sam Witteveen: VibeThinker 3B — taking on giant models
items.summary: Sam Witteveen reviews Weibo's VibeThinker-3B, the new 3B-parameter reasoning model that scores 80.2% on LiveCodeBench v6. Witteveen walks through how a 3B open-weights model competes with much larger frontier models on code and math reasoning.

explainer.tagline: Sam Witteveen's hands-on walkthrough of Weibo's VibeThinker-3B, the small open-weights reasoning model hitting 80.2% on LiveCodeBench v6.
explainer.whatIsIt: Sam Witteveen is a long-time AI YouTuber known for hands-on reviews of new open-weights models. In this video Sam Witteveen breaks down VibeThinker-3B, Weibo's 3-billion-parameter reasoning model that scores 80.2% on LiveCodeBench v6 — a result usually reserved for frontier-scale models.
explainer.howItWorks: Sam Witteveen runs through what makes VibeThinker-3B punch above its size: the training recipe, the reasoning behavior, and head-to-head comparisons against larger open and closed models on code and math tasks. The video shows how VibeThinker-3B performs at 3B parameters, which makes the model practical to run on a single consumer GPU.
explainer.whyItMatters: If a 3B model can hit 80.2% on LiveCodeBench v6, the cost-per-token math for hobbyists and small teams shifts. Sam Witteveen's review helps practitioners decide whether VibeThinker-3B is a serious daily driver or a benchmark-overfit curiosity, and shows where the gaps versus larger frontier models actually appear.
explainer.forWho: Local-model practitioners, indie devs evaluating small open-weights reasoning models

image.alt: Sam Witteveen YouTube thumbnail for VibeThinker 3B - Taking on Giant Models

items.title: MCP Enterprise-Managed Authorization — zero-touch OAuth for Claude, VS Code, Linear
items.summary: Enterprise-Managed Authorization, an MCP extension, is now stable. Admins provision MCP servers once through Okta and users get every connector on first login with no per-app OAuth. Claude, VS Code, Linear, Figma, Asana, Atlassian and Supabase ship support; Ramp is live with 2,000 users.

quickFacts.label: Protocol
quickFacts.value: Identity Assertion JWT Authorization Grant (ID-JAG) + OAuth

quickFacts.label: IdP support
quickFacts.value: Okta at launch (via Cross App Access)

quickFacts.label: Client support
quickFacts.value: Claude apps, Claude Code, Cowork, VS Code

quickFacts.label: MCP servers
quickFacts.value: Asana, Atlassian, Canva, Figma, Granola, Linear, Supabase, Slack

explainer.tagline: MCP's new Enterprise-Managed Authorization extension stabilizes — admins authorize MCP connectors once through Okta, users skip per-app OAuth.
explainer.whatIsIt: Enterprise-Managed Authorization (EMA) is an extension to the Model Context Protocol that just moved from draft to stable. EMA lets a company's identity provider — Okta at launch — grant access to MCP servers in bulk, scoped to user groups and roles. End users open Claude or VS Code, sign in once, and inherit every MCP connector their admin already approved without seeing an OAuth screen.
explainer.howItWorks: EMA leans on the Identity Assertion JWT Authorization Grant (ID-JAG) standard. During single sign-on, the MCP client receives an ID-JAG from the IdP and exchanges it for an access token at the MCP server, replacing the per-server consent screen. Admins enable specific MCP servers in the IdP, set group scopes, and audit usage through the same IdP logs they use for SaaS. Reduced token lifetimes let admins deprovision a leaving employee from every MCP server at once.
explainer.whyItMatters: Per-user OAuth on every MCP server is the main reason MCP has not landed in regulated enterprises — security teams want a single audit log and a single revoke button. With EMA stable, an admin can plug an MCP server into Okta the same way they plug in any SaaS, and rollouts the size of Ramp's 2,000-employee deployment become routine. The first wave of supported MCP servers (Asana, Atlassian, Canva, Figma, Linear, Supabase) covers most enterprise workflow tools.
explainer.forWho: Enterprise IT, security teams, MCP server maintainers

image.alt: Model Context Protocol blog announcement card for Enterprise-Managed Authorization

faq.q: What is MCP Enterprise-Managed Authorization?
faq.a: Enterprise-Managed Authorization (EMA) is a Model Context Protocol extension, announced stable on June 18, 2026, that lets an organization's identity provider grant access to MCP servers in bulk. Admins enable an MCP connector once in Okta; users sign in once and inherit access to every approved server, replacing per-server OAuth consent screens with a centrally audited flow.

faq.q: Which clients and MCP servers support EMA at launch?
faq.a: Anthropic ships EMA in Claude apps, Claude Code, and Cowork, plus support in Visual Studio Code. MCP servers from Asana, Atlassian, Canva, Figma, Granola, Linear, and Supabase ship support at launch, with Slack listed as in progress. Okta is the first supported identity provider, via the Cross App Access protocol.

faq.q: How does Enterprise-Managed Authorization work under the hood?
faq.a: EMA uses the Identity Assertion JWT Authorization Grant (ID-JAG) standard. During single sign-on, an MCP client obtains an ID-JAG from the identity provider and exchanges it for an MCP server access token. The MCP server trusts tokens minted via the admin-approved IdP rather than running its own consent UI, so users do not see per-server OAuth prompts.

faq.q: What enterprise problems does Enterprise-Managed Authorization solve?
faq.a: EMA gives security teams a single grant-and-revoke surface for every MCP connector instead of one OAuth grant per user per server. Admins set short token lifetimes for fast deprovisioning when employees leave, route audit logs through the IdP, and can force MCP connectors to use only the corporate IdP so personal and work accounts stay separate. Ramp reports 2,000 employees rolled out through Okta with no extra steps.

items.notes: On HN front page (190 points). Major protocol-level shift for enterprise MCP adoption.

items.title: OpenAI Codex Record & Replay — demonstrate a macOS workflow, get a reusable skill
items.summary: OpenAI Codex App 26.616 adds Record & Replay on macOS. Run a workflow once and Codex saves it as an editable, reusable skill that replays through Computer Use, browser tools, and plugins. Requires Computer Use; not in the EEA, UK, or Switzerland at launch.

quickFacts.label: Availability
quickFacts.value: Not in EEA, UK, or Switzerland at launch

quickFacts.label: What's new
quickFacts.value: Demonstrate a workflow once → Codex saves it as a reusable, editable skill

explainer.tagline: OpenAI Codex 26.616 lets you record a Mac workflow once, then replay it as a reusable agent skill.
explainer.whatIsIt: Record & Replay is a new feature in the OpenAI Codex desktop app, shipped on June 18, 2026 in version 26.616. The user demonstrates a workflow on their Mac — clicks, typing, window switches — and Codex captures the actions and visible window content. Codex turns the recording into an inspectable, editable skill that the agent can replay later through Computer Use, browser tools, and installed plugins.
explainer.howItWorks: After starting a recording, Codex watches both the actions taken and the surrounding window state, then synthesizes a skill the agent can run on its own. Each skill is editable after capture: prompts, branches, and action steps can be refined without re-recording. Record & Replay rides on top of Codex's existing Computer Use capability, so the resulting skill can drive native macOS apps, the in-app browser, and any plugin already installed.
explainer.whyItMatters: Codex pitches Record & Replay for tasks that are 'easier to show than to describe' — filing an expense report, submitting a time-off request, running a recurring data export. Spelling those out in a prompt was the friction point that pushed many repetitive workflows out of Computer Use; demonstrating them is faster, and the resulting skill is reviewable instead of a black-box recording.
explainer.forWho: macOS users of the Codex desktop app running Computer Use workflows
explainer.tryIt: Update Codex App to 26.616 on macOS, enable Computer Use, then open Record & Replay from the menu.

image.alt: OpenAI Codex Record and Replay feature page social card

faq.q: What is OpenAI Codex Record & Replay?
faq.a: Record & Replay is a macOS feature added to the OpenAI Codex desktop app on June 18, 2026 in Codex App 26.616. The user demonstrates a workflow once — clicks, typing, window switches — and Codex turns the recorded actions plus visible window content into a reusable agent skill. The skill can be edited after capture and replayed through Computer Use, browser tools, and installed plugins.

faq.q: What does Codex Record & Replay require to run?
faq.a: Record & Replay runs on macOS only and requires Computer Use to be available and enabled in the Codex app. The feature is excluded from the European Economic Area, the United Kingdom, and Switzerland at launch. The macOS version that introduced Record & Replay is Codex App 26.616, shipped June 18, 2026.

faq.q: How is Record & Replay different from a regular Codex prompt?
faq.a: A normal Codex prompt describes the goal in writing; Record & Replay captures the sequence of actions as you demonstrate them. The resulting skill is an inspectable, editable artifact that Codex can replay later — useful when the workflow is repetitive, depends on personal preferences, or is easier to show than to spell out step by step.

items.notes: Official OpenAI Codex feature page, dated June 18 in the changelog. Within 72h.

items.title: Simon Willison: GLM-5.2 is probably the most powerful text-only open weights LLM
items.summary: Simon Willison calls Z.ai's GLM-5.2 today's strongest open-weights text LLM: top of Artificial Analysis Intelligence Index v4.1 at 51, second on Code Arena WebDev behind Claude Fable 5, and ~$1.40/$4.40 per 1M tokens on OpenRouter vs GPT-5.5's $5/$30.

explainer.tagline: Simon Willison ranks GLM-5.2 as today's top open-weights text LLM — frontier-class scores at roughly a quarter of GPT-5.5's price.
explainer.whatIsIt: Simon Willison's June 17 post argues that Z.ai's recently released GLM-5.2 — a 753B-parameter Mixture-of-Experts model under MIT license with a 1M-token context window — is currently the strongest text-only open-weights LLM. Willison points to Artificial Analysis Intelligence Index v4.1, where GLM-5.2 ranks first among open weights with a score of 51, and to Code Arena's WebDev leaderboard, where GLM-5.2 sits second behind only Claude Fable 5.
explainer.howItWorks: Willison runs GLM-5.2 through OpenRouter at roughly $1.40 per million input tokens and $4.40 per million output tokens — meaningfully cheaper than GPT-5.5 ($5/$30) or Claude Opus 4.5–4.8 ($5/$25). He notes one tradeoff flagged by Artificial Analysis: GLM-5.2 uses about 43k output tokens per task versus 26k for GLM-5.1, which dents the cost advantage on long agent runs. Willison also reruns his SVG benchmarks — the pelican-on-bicycle improves over GLM-5.1, but the opossum-on-e-scooter regresses.
explainer.whyItMatters: If you can run a 1.51 TB checkpoint or are happy on OpenRouter, GLM-5.2 closes most of the gap to the closed frontier on coding and general intelligence at a quarter to a sixth of the price. Simon Willison's framing matters because it sets the open-weights conversation: GLM-5.2 is now the default candidate for 'self-host or cheap API.' The increased token spend is the catch buyers should price in.
explainer.forWho: AI engineers comparing open-weights models, OpenRouter and self-host users, cost-sensitive teams

image.alt: Simon Willison social card for GLM-5.2 post showing his SVG pelican benchmark

items.notes: Top-list influential voice; substantive standalone analysis of GLM-5.2 economics and benchmarks.

items.title: Wes Roth: Google's 'POST AGI' paper — DeepMind's AGI-to-ASI roadmap
items.summary: Wes Roth breaks down 'From AGI to ASI', a Google DeepMind paper co-signed by Shane Legg, Marcus Hutter and Thore Graepel that maps four routes from human-level AI to superintelligence.

explainer.tagline: Wes Roth walks through 'From AGI to ASI', the new DeepMind report on what comes after human-level AI.
explainer.whatIsIt: A YouTube breakdown by creator Wes Roth of the new Google DeepMind paper 'From AGI to ASI' (arXiv 2606.12683), submitted June 10. The paper is co-signed by Shane Legg, Marcus Hutter, Thore Graepel, Tim Genewein and 10 others, and defines AGI as a system that performs at typical-human level across most cognitive tasks.
explainer.howItWorks: The DeepMind paper names four pathways from AGI to ASI: scaling compute, models and training data; discovering new architectures and paradigms; using AI to recursively accelerate AI research; and coordinating large groups of AI agents into collectively superhuman systems. Wes Roth walks each route, the assumptions behind it, and the timelines DeepMind sketches.
explainer.whyItMatters: When DeepMind's Shane Legg, one of the field's longest-serving AGI researchers, co-authors a 57-page roadmap for what happens AFTER AGI, the framing shapes how labs, governments and investors think about post-AGI risk and policy through 2026.
explainer.forWho: people tracking AGI safety, post-AGI policy and lab roadmaps

image.alt: Wes Roth YouTube thumbnail for 'Google's SHOCKING POST AGI paper'

items.title: LifeSciBench — OpenAI's 750-task benchmark for life-science research
items.summary: OpenAI's LifeSciBench grades AI models on 750 expert-authored life-science tasks using rubrics. The strongest model, GPT-Rosalind, passes only 36.1%, with attached data files cited as the main bottleneck.

faq.q: What is LifeSciBench?
faq.a: LifeSciBench is OpenAI's 750-task evaluation that grades how well AI models handle real life-science research. Each task uses an expert-written rubric instead of multiple choice, and the benchmark spans seven biological domains and seven research workflows.

faq.q: How well do current models do on LifeSciBench?
faq.a: LifeSciBench's strongest model is GPT-Rosalind at 36.1% task pass rate (rubric score 0.576). GPT-5.5 reaches 25.7%, Gemini 3.1 Pro 23.6%, GPT-5.4 20.7%, and Grok 4.3 13.0%. OpenAI says the benchmark is far from saturated.

faq.q: Why is LifeSciBench harder than older biology benchmarks?
faq.a: LifeSciBench uses free-response tasks graded by 19,020 rubric criteria written by 173 PhD scientists, not multiple-choice answers. About 53% of tasks require models to interpret attached artifacts like figures, PDFs, sequence files, and chemical structures.

faq.q: What's the biggest weakness LifeSciBench exposed?
faq.a: Artifact processing is the main bottleneck. GPT-Rosalind's pass rate drops 17 points from 45.1% on text-only tasks to 28.1% on tasks with attached data files, showing that current models struggle with scientific figures and structured biology files.

explainer.tagline: OpenAI's new biology benchmark grades models with expert rubrics, and even GPT-Rosalind passes only one task in three.
explainer.whatIsIt: LifeSciBench is OpenAI's new evaluation suite for AI in life-science research. It contains 750 free-response tasks across seven biology workflows and seven domains, written by 173 PhD-level scientists from biotech and pharma. Each task is graded by a detailed rubric instead of a single right answer.
explainer.howItWorks: LifeSciBench grades model output with 19,020 expert-written criteria — about 25 per task — and tracks two scores: a normalized rubric score and a task pass rate at the 70% threshold. About 53% of LifeSciBench tasks include attached artifacts (figures, PDFs, sequence files, chemical structures, tables) that models must read, and 79% need multi-step reasoning averaging four decision steps.
explainer.whyItMatters: LifeSciBench gives biotech teams a hard, realistic yardstick for picking models to run scientific workflows. OpenAI's leaderboard shows even the strongest model (GPT-Rosalind, 36.1% pass rate) is far from saturating the benchmark, and that file-handling — not text reasoning — is where current models break down.
explainer.forWho: AI eval researchers, biotech and pharma teams deploying LLMs
explainer.tryIt: Read the LifeSciBench paper (PDF) on cdn.openai.com

image.alt: LifeSciBench announcement card — OpenAI's 750-task life-science benchmark

items.title: ChatGPT Scheduled Tasks Hub — sidebar page replaces Pulse
items.summary: ChatGPT now has a dedicated Scheduled page in the sidebar where users can view, pause, edit, or delete recurring prompts and monitoring tasks. The launch replaces Pulse, which OpenAI retires in 14 days.

quickFacts.label: Available to

explainer.tagline: ChatGPT moves scheduled prompts into a single sidebar Scheduled page and retires Pulse.
explainer.whatIsIt: ChatGPT Scheduled Tasks is OpenAI's redesigned home for recurring prompts inside ChatGPT. A new Scheduled page in the sidebar lists every active task with its next run time and lets users pause, resume, edit, or delete it without scrolling through chat threads.
explainer.howItWorks: Scheduled Tasks runs each saved prompt on its own cadence — at a specific time or a broader window like morning, afternoon, or evening — capped at once per hour. Monitoring tasks can search the web and check connected apps, then notify the user only when something material changes. Unattended tasks may pause automatically after a period of inactivity.

items.title: Wolfram Language 15 — Mathematica adds built-in AI Assistant and Agent Tools
items.summary: Wolfram Language 15 ships a built-in AI Assistant inside every notebook and a new Wolfram Agent Tools framework that lets Claude Code and other agent environments call Mathematica functions and read or write notebooks.

explainer.tagline: Wolfram Language 15 ships an in-notebook AI Assistant plus an Agent Tools framework that exposes Mathematica to Claude Code and other coding agents.
explainer.whatIsIt: Wolfram Language 15 is the new release of Stephen Wolfram's symbolic computing system and Mathematica notebook environment. The headline change is AI: every notebook now ships with an AI Assistant available at the Basic level and a 'Chatbar' that puts a chat cell at the bottom of new notebooks by default.
explainer.howItWorks: The AI Assistant has Basic, Pro, and Research tiers — Basic answers Wolfram Language questions out of the box, Pro handles larger projects, and Research adds the latest frontier models. The new Wolfram Agent Tools framework registers Mathematica with external coding agents like Claude Code, so the agent can evaluate Wolfram Language code, read and write notebooks, and call Wolfram functions automatically when it needs them.
explainer.whyItMatters: Wolfram Language 15 turns Mathematica into a callable tool for AI coding agents and gives existing Mathematica users an in-product AI helper. Researchers and engineers running symbolic math, plots, or data analysis can now drive those workflows from inside Claude Code or directly from a chat cell in the notebook.
explainer.forWho: Mathematica users, researchers, and Claude Code users who want symbolic math and data analysis tools
explainer.tryIt: Download Wolfram Language 15 from wolfram.com or open the new Chatbar in any v15 notebook
